speaker: interviewer
text: I'm speaking to Cissie Nathan, and this is tape 2.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: I want you to go back with me to the day when the Nazis entered Holland. You were standing on the street corner with your doctor and you went back into your house. Describe what was the conversation, what was the mood, what did you people-- what did you the family do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't living near the family then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my family. At that moment, the children were still in school, so I went to get the children when it was time but didn't know what was happening everywhere else with the Jewish people. We knew nothing about that. So we thought, well, we just have to carry on the way we were carrying on. My husband had to work and we did that. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a conversation that night when your husband came home? Did you discuss anything about the invasion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, of course we felt terrible that we were occupied and we wondered, you know, what would happen. And we chatted about it for a while, but then I said to my husband, I said, you know something? I said, we should have gone. He said-- but I remember, he said to my mother at the time when she came over, he said, I can't leave my mother and father. So she said, the house is big enough, they can come. But of course they wouldn't-- they wouldn't go, leave their family. So none of us went, as I said before. Then we were on--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me-- I'm sorry-- you were telling me that you were in [INAUDIBLE], that you had [INAUDIBLE]. You were saved from deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had the special [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was a [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, if they picked you up in the street, they didn't have to take you. So that's what we had, and we carried it with us. Then they-- because by that time they brought in that we had to wear the Star of David. So we all wore them. So naturally they started picking people up off the streets. And then I can't remember the-- [SOBBING] the exact-- the exact year when we heard that the-- my in-laws had been picked up. Didn't know where they'd gone or anything, my mother-in-law and father-in-law. But my two brothers-in-law and their wives and family were still around for a while. But gradually-- and they lived in the-- all lived in the Jewish quarter, so gradually they were all taken. But I had a-- the lady that helped me was a-- and she helped me in the new address. She was non-Jewish, of course. In the end, they stopped them coming, so she didn't come anymore. But she was very good. She used to come at 12 o'clock at night and bring me some apples or something for the children. She saved some of my things for me, and I got them back after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you stop her from coming? You said you stopped her--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't stop her, the Germans stopped her from coming. They weren't allowed to mix with Jewish people anymore. No. And then they--

speaker: interviewer
text: You moved into the Jewish quarters, or you lived in them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the second time. When-- when we moved from the Jewish quarter, it was a non-Jewish quarter. It was a new area in Amsterdam, Amsterdam South. And we were all, you know, Jews and Gentiles, all mixed. We lived in the same road and that. There was no trouble before. None at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the anti-Jewish laws came out, how did it start and what did you have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I said, we were on rations, naturally. And we were only allowed to go to the shops between 3:00 and 5:00. So naturally, a lot of the things had already gone. But that's why the lady who helped me before brought me things for the children that they couldn't have. And we weren't allowed-- my late brother-in-law, who had the-- he had a big butcher shop well known in Amsterdam, and I used to get the meat there. But we weren't allowed into that anymore. So you didn't get any meat. But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my sister's husband, who-- my second sister, the one under me, she came to Holland for holidays. And she met a friend of Maurice's father. Because my-- Maurice's father had three friends, all Jewish, and they used to play bridge one night a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my sister came for holiday, she met them all. So then she got engaged to the friend. And also married in Holland and lived in Holland near where I lived with my husband and the children in the new quarter. Yeah. Did she also have [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because he was working for the Jewish-- what do they call it-- -rat? The--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The-- yeah. He was working for them. So they had [INAUDIBLE] like till the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the day when you first put on your yellow star and walked out on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we first put-- had to put the star on, we felt-- one way proud to have it and another time disconsolate, you know, upset and wondered then what was going to happen. We had to move in the end from where we lived back to a Jewish quarter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we were allowed to take everything from where we lived to the new place. And that's where we were taken on the 20th of June, 1943? '43. We were one of the last groups to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first wore the yellow star and had to go out on the street, were you afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. We weren't afraid. No. We weren't afraid to go out in the street with it at that time till they started picking people up. Then we got afraid.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the news that people are being picked up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the news that people are being picked up on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: News travels fast. But we-- I don't remember how we heard it but heard it from someone probably non-Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because they were allowed to go everywhere and anywhere. So probably that's how it happened. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they picked up most of the Jews, how did you feel living still in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- we realized that some time or other-- because we'd been moved to the Jewish quarter-- we'd be picked up. We realized that. But in a way, because we belonged to the diamond group as such, we were allowed to take one suitcase of our own clothes with us. Yes. They'd already taken--

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did that happen? When did you find out that you also will have to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only when they came to fetch us in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you warned ahead of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, no, no. But we knew what was happening, and we knew-- we realized we would be picked up. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the children understand what was going down?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, they didn't. My children didn't understand at that time where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. Maurice was-- Maurice was nearly 11, and Helen was dated two years younger, nine and 11, yeah. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to Westerbork, a camp in Holland. And we were there together with our own clothes and things, with all the other people belonging to the group, till we were transferred to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported to Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was ordinary-- ordinary train, I think I remember. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Passenger train? Regular passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Regular passengers, men and women of the group, of course. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in their group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't quite remember. I think there were about 168 women and men, all married. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I can't really remember how many, but my son would know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe when you arrived to Westerbork. What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Westerbork, we had, in the beginning, no thought that we'd be transferred anywhere else. But then, people that were there that we knew were taken. And then we realized that they were going somewhere else. And they were told that they were going to Germany to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Westerbork? Were those houses or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was in barracks. We-- in Westerbork, we were in barracks. The boys went with the fathers, and the girls stayed with the mothers. But we used to see each other every day, every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they had intentions of building a diamond factory in Belsen, where we were sent to. And I believe the apparatus is still there. I'm not sure, but I believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you arrived to Westerbork, did they immediately separate you from your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: For sleeping, yeah. For sleeping, yes. And those that had little children, like my sister. Johnny was very young, about four. They stayed to look after the children, and we were set to work. My husband worked there. I don't remember what he did. I don't remember what he did, but I was sent with the other women to a factory. There were all benches and seats. And we sat there, and we had to strip boots and shoes, leather boots and shoes, and put the pieces all together. And I believe they were sent to Germany later. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but-- but the work I did wasn't in Westerbork. The work I did was in-- oh, see, I'm muddled them. Sorry. The work I did was when I got to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Westerbork, oh, I-- I worked in the kitchen in Westerbork, just preparing, you know, washing things for us to eat. They used-- they dished us up one day spinach with all the maggots in it because you couldn't separate them. Well, who could eat it? Nobody could eat it, but we weren't allowed to throw it away. So we waited until they were gone and then put it down the drain. And we used to get a small piece of bread, about 2 inches.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Westerbork, you had a community kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: A community kitchen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all. We had nothing. We had a stove in the barrack. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody cooked for themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Where were we getting the food? There was no food. Only what-- in Westerbork-- oh. I forget. We must have had some food in Westerbork, but I forget what it-- what it was. And-- and there was one group, one couple, husband and wife with four children, had brought little bits of candle from home. And when it was all clear, somebody used to stand outside the barracks to see that they weren't coming and used to light them on Friday night. Yeah. Their name was [? Fauss, ?] [? Fauss. ?] And because one child of theirs had been born in England, they weren't sent to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you realize that the children behaved differently in Westerbork? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They were all right together because there were more children. But the same family that lit the candles in Westerbork lit them in-- where we were transferred to Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any activities for the children in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really. No. Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they do all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Play around the barracks. Yeah. Play around the-- oh, yes. I think I brought some pencils and paper for them. I think I remember that. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long did you stay in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed in Westerbork till I think-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I can't remember. My son would remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Months or-- or-- or years?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not a year. Less than a year. Must have been months, six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they take you from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us to Belsen from Westerbork, and that's where I had the spinach with-- with the-- Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Belsen, the whole family together?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belsen, we were together.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. From Westerbork, the family went together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. We all went together. The whole group went together to Belsen. And there we were separated.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you transported to Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. By train, I believe-- I think we were. We weren't in the goods wagons then. We were only in the goods wagons when we left Belsen. Yeah. By train, we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in passenger-- passenger train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Belsen in a passenger train. Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your arrival to Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the way from Holland to Belsen, we passed Hamburg, and all the blinds were down. But there was a crack in one of them. And we saw a bit of Hamburg, and it was flattened. And we said, well, that's a good-- [LAUGHS] a good thing that happened. I remember saying that. And it-- in a way, maybe it was wicked for the people, but by that time, they were bombing England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that they were bombing England?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know that till after the war. I didn't know that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened in Belsen when you arrived.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when we got to Belsen, we were split up. Boys with their fathers, and girls with their mothers-- split up again. And then the Germans had something wrong with their counting every day. We had to get out of the barrack 5 o'clock in the morning sometimes, sometimes in the snow, to be counted. And they could never count because we used to stand there for hours. One morning my daughter-- God rest her soul-- collapsed, and I was allowed to take her into the barracks. But that was all. Then I had to come out again because those-- there were a few babies. So they stayed in the barracks. So they counted them in the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The diamond group.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you remain together in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always together. Yes. Always together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the children remained with the parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we had our suitcases still. Yeah. We still had our suitcases because at that time, they thought of making a factory there. So we were all together, and we had our own clothes there. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day. Every day. Every day. They had to work. I don't know what work he did, but we would see him every day. Every day. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did the children do here in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you-- you must ask my son what he did because during the day I had to go and get something ready for the next day, you know. I didn't do it. I had to clean whatever they gave us to eat. Yeah. It was never meat or anything like that. Nothing. And we had piece of bread about 2 inches. We used to get ersatz coffee, I think. American ersatz coffee we used to get. Yeah. And that was about it. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any-- any cooked food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No cooked food that I remember. No. No. But my son remembers more than I do because I've become a lot of years, so quite forgetful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you related to your little girl during this period of time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were-- we were always very close, all of us. The children, yeah. Very close. And my little nephew, who was four years old, always very, very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she complain a lot?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, not at all. I will say this-- God rest her soul. Even in Amsterdam when she was little, I had trouble with her with her food. And the children's doctor used to say to me, don't worry because when she's hungry, she'll eat. But my son Maurice was always a good eater, always.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Belsen, did she have trouble eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: What there was, no. There was very little so she could-- she ate that. [SIGHS] You were allowed to receive parcels in Belsen. My mother-- God rest her soul-- said we were allowed one Red Cross letter, which I sent, as I'd thought, to my mother. But she'd passed away that I didn't know. But my sister answered it. And when my mother knew that-- she passed away later-- when my mother knew that, she sent Red Cross parcels to all of us. But we'd already left. So my sister got them, but the cigarettes and chocolate and that-- she told me after the war-- they'd taken out. But she did get them. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister went with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister went with you to Bergen-Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Belsen with her husband and a little boy, my nephew who-- John, who lives in London with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said in the-- getting the-- washing food and washing food for the SS. But we had none of that. None of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister worked with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was with the children. She stayed in the barracks looking after the children because Johnny was only four years old. Yeah. And there were young children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who was picked to work, and who was picked to stay home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those that they thought could work had to go to work, and those that had young children stayed home. Otherwise, we worked in a place where we had to cut good leather away from shoes and boots. Afterwards, we realized who-- [SOBBING] who those boots and shoes belonged to. [CRYING] I'm sorry. I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Don't be.

speaker: interviewee
text: All our-- at home all went to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We weren't allowed to take anything away from it, as I told you before. But the SS used to come up and take something out the drawer that you couldn't say anything. And they used to come up at night-- when we were in the Jewish quarter-- and see if you were there and-- because some-- a lot of Jewish people went into hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: A lot of Jewish people went into hiding in Amsterdam, those that had money to pay. And I heard later-- later that, when the money was up, some of them used to give them up to the Germans. So they went to the camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What can you say? And I'm scared now. I'm even scared now what's happening in Israel. That frightens me.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war, my sister and I were in England again. We thought we'd take-- go into Palestine-- Israel then-- with the two-- with the two boys. But my grandmother was still alive, and my family were there. And not only that, we decided if they had to-- if we went to Israel, they'd have to join the army. And they'd had enough. So we decided to stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to Belsen. What-- what did you know about the rest of the population in Belsen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd-- in Belsen? Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that there was another camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew nothing about that. Nothing. When we were in Belsen, they used to say the German people never knew anything about it? That was a big lie. Because across the road were houses. They must've known.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the civilian population? Did you see German--

speaker: interviewee
text: We only saw civilian population when we were in Hamburg, when they-- they were losing the war, the Germans. Up. That's what I meant before. I shouldn't have said Westerbork. That's when we saw the people living in houses across the road, and we were in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who took care of you in Belsen? Who were the guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Belsen? The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. The SS. The SS.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that Irma Grese, she was hung after the war, she was there. Yes. And I don't-- that was the one I remember. She was worse than some of the men.
